text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Ananya Patel 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Kingston Community Hospital, Galsworthy Road, Kingston upon Thames, KT2 7QB  

Dear Dr Ananya Patel, 

Mrs Evelyn Hart, DOB: 22-03-1938, Hospital No: KCH-402781 NHS No. 9430021876  
Flat 2, Rosewood Care Home, 14 Meadow Lane, Surbiton, KT5 8PA  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent episodes of transient impaired awareness with morning predominance; exact classification remains undetermined

Aetiology: 

Uncertain; background cerebrovascular small vessel disease on prior community imaging report (not available today) Ã‚Â· further evaluation planned

Other Medical Problems: 

Hypertension on Amlodipine 5 mg daily; Type 2 diabetes diet-controlled; Chronic osteoarthritis of knees and hips; Reduced mobility with bedbound status since December 2024; Stage 2 chronic kidney disease; Mild cognitive impairment ï¿½ under community memory clinic surveillance

Management Plan: 

- Continue current antiseizure therapy as per caregiver medication administration record: Levetiracetam 500 mg mane and 500 mg nocte; no recent missed doses reported by nurse.  
- Arrange community EEG with video if feasible at bedside; if not, schedule ambulatory EEG.  
- Request metabolic screen (U&Es, Ca/Mg/PO4, LFTs), HbA1c, vitamin D, FBC, and Levetiracetam trough level prior to morning dose.  
- Implement seizure diary template with caregiver sign-off; document timing, duration, provocation, recovery phase, and injuries.  
- Falls risk review by community therapy team; pressure area care to continue.  
- Driving not applicable (bedbound).  
- Telephone review in six weeks with nurse present; face-to-face domiciliary visit if events escalate. 

This bedbound older adult was reviewed with the live-in nurse/caregiver, who provided the majority of the history and observations. Over the last four weeks the caregiver reports four clusters this month; each ≈five absences in the morning, typically between 06:30 and 09:00, each brief at 10–30 seconds with vacant staring, unresponsiveness, and subtle oral automatisms. There is no clear tonic-clonic activity, tongue biting, or incontinence. Post-event there is mild confusion lasting up to five minutes, after which the patient returns to baseline drowsiness. No events noted in the afternoon or overnight per hourly rounding charts. Appetite is variable, fluids adequate, and there have been no intercurrent infections. The nurse has noted that events tend to occur on days with poor sleep and when morning cares are delayed. 

Examination today at bedside showed the patient comfortable, afebrile, and haemodynamically stable (BP 134/72 mmHg, HR 68 regular). Cranial nerves grossly intact; speech soft but coherent; no lateralising limb weakness though limited by pain from osteoarthritis. No tremor or myoclonus observed. Skin intact with prophylactic dressings in situ Ã©. 

I discussed with the caregiver practical measures including consistent morning routines, ensuring on-time medication administration, and minimising sleep disruption. We will review results and event logs at the planned follow-up. If any prolonged episode (>5 minutes) or a cluster with reduced recovery occurs, they will call 999 as per care plan â€˜red flagâ€™ guidance. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the last four weeks the caregiver reports four clusters this month; each ≈five absences in the morning
four clusters this month; each ≈five absences in the morning

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "Over the last four weeks the caregiver reports four clusters this month; each ≈five absences in the morning". This provides a clear frequency over a month and specifies number per cluster. No other conflicting frequencies are mentioned. Therefore, normalize to '<clusters per month>, <events per cluster>'.
seizure_frequency_number: Over the last four weeks the caregiver reports four clusters this month; each ≈five absences in the morning
reference: four clusters this month; each ≈five absences in the morning
